question_text: Express {tex}\tan ^{-1}( \frac{\cos x}{1-\sin x}),-\frac{3 \pi}{2}<x<\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} in the simplest form.
question_html: Express {tex}\tan ^{-1}( \frac{\cos x}{1-\sin x}),-\frac{3 \pi}{2}<x<\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} in the simplest form.
solution_text: According to question, we have {tex}\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\cos x}{1-\sin x}\right){/tex} = {tex}\tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{\cos ^{2} \frac{x}{2}-\sin ^{2} \frac{x}{2}}{\cos ^{2} \frac{x}{2}+\sin ^{2} \frac{x}{2}-2 \sin \frac{x}{2} \cos \frac{x}{2}}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{\left(\cos \frac{x}{2}+\sin \frac{x}{2}\right)\left(\cos \frac{x}{2}-\sin \frac{x}{2}\right)}{\left(\cos \frac{x}{2}-\sin \frac{x}{2}\right)^{2}}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{\cos \frac{x}{2}+\sin \frac{x}{2}}{\cos \frac{x}{2}-\sin \frac{x}{2}}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{1+\tan \frac{x}{2}}{1-\tan \frac{x}{2}}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\tan ^{-1}\left[\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}+\frac{x}{2}\right)\right]=\frac{\pi}{4}+\frac{x}{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
According to question, we have 
{tex}\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\cos x}{1-\sin x}\right){/tex} = {tex}\tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{\cos ^{2} \frac{x}{2}-\sin ^{2} \frac{x}{2}}{\cos ^{2} \frac{x}{2}+\sin ^{2} \frac{x}{2}-2 \sin \frac{x}{2} \cos \frac{x}{2}}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{\left(\cos \frac{x}{2}+\sin \frac{x}{2}\right)\left(\cos \frac{x}{2}-\sin \frac{x}{2}\right)}{\left(\cos \frac{x}{2}-\sin \frac{x}{2}\right)^{2}}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{\cos \frac{x}{2}+\sin \frac{x}{2}}{\cos \frac{x}{2}-\sin \frac{x}{2}}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{1+\tan \frac{x}{2}}{1-\tan \frac{x}{2}}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\tan ^{-1}\left[\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}+\frac{x}{2}\right)\right]=\frac{\pi}{4}+\frac{x}{2}{/tex}

question_text: Find the principal value of tan -1 (-1).
solution_text: The given inverse trignometric function is {tex}\tan ^ { - 1 } ( - 1 ){/tex} Now, {tex}\tan ^ { - 1 } ( - 1 ) = \tan ^ { - 1 } \left[ - \tan (\frac { \pi } { 4 } \right)]{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \tan \frac { \pi } { 4 } = 1 \right]{/tex} {tex}= \tan ^ { - 1 } \left[ \tan \left( - \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right) \right]{/tex} {tex}[ \because - \tan \theta = \tan ( - \theta ) ]{/tex} {tex}= - \frac { \pi } { 4 } \left[ \because \tan ^ { - 1 } ( \tan \theta ) = \theta ; \forall \theta \in \left( \frac { - \pi } { 2 } , \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) \right]{/tex} which is the required principal value.
solution_html:
The given inverse trignometric function is {tex}\tan ^ { - 1 } ( - 1 ){/tex}
Now, {tex}\tan ^ { - 1 } ( - 1 ) = \tan ^ { - 1 } \left[ - \tan (\frac { \pi } { 4 } \right)]{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \tan \frac { \pi } { 4 } = 1 \right]{/tex}
{tex}= \tan ^ { - 1 } \left[ \tan \left( - \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right) \right]{/tex} {tex}[ \because - \tan \theta = \tan ( - \theta ) ]{/tex}
{tex}= - \frac { \pi } { 4 } \left[ \because \tan ^ { - 1 } ( \tan \theta ) = \theta ; \forall \theta \in \left( \frac { - \pi } { 2 } , \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) \right]{/tex}
which is the required principal value.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the value of tan –1 (1) + cos –1 {tex}(-\frac{1}{2})+\sin ^{-1}(-\frac{1}{2}){/tex}
solution_text: Let us consider tan -1 (1) = x then we obtain tan x = 1 = tan {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} We know that range of the principle value branch of tan -1 is {tex}\left[-\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right]{/tex} Thus, tan -1 (1) = {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} Let {tex}\cos ^{-1}\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right)=y{/tex} cos y = {tex}-\frac{1}{2}=\cos \left(\pi-\frac{\pi}{3}\right)=\cos \left(\frac{2 \pi}{3}\right){/tex} We know that range of the principle value branch of cos -1 is [0, {tex}\pi{/tex} ] Thus, {tex}\cos ^{-1}\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right)=\frac{2 \pi}{3}{/tex} Let sin -1 {tex}\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right)=z{/tex} {tex}\sin z=-\sin \frac{\pi}{6}=\sin \left(-\frac{\pi}{6}\right){/tex} We know that range of the principle value branch of sin -1 is {tex}\left[-\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right]{/tex} Thus, sin -1 {tex}\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right)=-\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex} Now,we have {tex}\tan ^{-1}(1)+\cos ^{-1}\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right)+\sin \left(-\frac{1}{2}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}+\frac{2 \pi}{3}-\frac{\pi}{6}=\frac{3 \pi+8 \pi-2 \pi}{12}=\frac{9 \pi}{12}=\frac{3 \pi}{4}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let us consider tan-1(1) = x then we obtain
tan x = 1 = tan{tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} 
We know that range of the principle value branch of tan-1 is {tex}\left[-\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right]{/tex}
Thus, tan-1 (1) = {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
Let {tex}\cos ^{-1}\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right)=y{/tex}
cos y = {tex}-\frac{1}{2}=\cos \left(\pi-\frac{\pi}{3}\right)=\cos \left(\frac{2 \pi}{3}\right){/tex}
We know that range of the principle value branch of cos-1 is [0, {tex}\pi{/tex}]
Thus, {tex}\cos ^{-1}\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right)=\frac{2 \pi}{3}{/tex}
Let sin-1 {tex}\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right)=z{/tex}
{tex}\sin z=-\sin \frac{\pi}{6}=\sin \left(-\frac{\pi}{6}\right){/tex}
We know that range of the principle value branch of sin-1 is {tex}\left[-\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right]{/tex}
Thus, sin-1 {tex}\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right)=-\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex}
Now,we have
{tex}\tan ^{-1}(1)+\cos ^{-1}\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right)+\sin \left(-\frac{1}{2}\right){/tex}
= {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}+\frac{2 \pi}{3}-\frac{\pi}{6}=\frac{3 \pi+8 \pi-2 \pi}{12}=\frac{9 \pi}{12}=\frac{3 \pi}{4}{/tex}

solution_text: Putting {tex}x = \tan \theta{/tex} and {tex}y = \tan \phi{/tex} {tex}\therefore \tan \frac{1}{2}\left[ {{{\sin }^{ - 1}}\frac{{2x}}{{1 + {x^2}}} + {{\cos }^{ - 1}}\frac{{1 - {y^2}}}{{1 + {y^2}}}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \tan \frac{1}{2}\left[ {{{\sin }^{ - 1}}\frac{{2\tan \theta }}{{1 + {{\tan }^2}\theta }} + {{\cos }^{ - 1}}\frac{{1 - {{\tan }^2}\phi }}{{1 + {{\tan }^2}\phi }}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \tan \frac{1}{2}\left[ {{{\sin }^{ - 1}}\sin 2\theta + {{\cos }^{ - 1}}\cos 2\phi } \right]{/tex} {tex} = \tan \frac{1}{2}\left[ {2\theta + 2\phi } \right]{/tex} {tex} = \tan \left[ {\theta + \phi } \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{\tan \theta + \tan \phi }}{{1 - \tan \theta \tan \phi }}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{x + y}}{{1 - xy}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Putting {tex}x = \tan \theta{/tex} and {tex}y = \tan \phi{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \tan \frac{1}{2}\left[ {{{\sin }^{ - 1}}\frac{{2x}}{{1 + {x^2}}} + {{\cos }^{ - 1}}\frac{{1 - {y^2}}}{{1 + {y^2}}}} \right]{/tex}

question_text: Find the value of the expression {tex}\tan \left( {{{\sin }^{ - 1}}\frac{3}{5} + {{\cot }^{ - 1}}\frac{3}{2}} \right){/tex} .
question_html: Find the value of the expression {tex}\tan \left( {{{\sin }^{ - 1}}\frac{3}{5} + {{\cot }^{ - 1}}\frac{3}{2}} \right){/tex}.
solution_text: Putting, {tex}{\sin ^{ - 1}}\frac{3}{5} = x{/tex} and {tex}{\cot ^{ - 1}}\frac{3}{2} = y{/tex} so that {tex}\sin x = \frac{3}{5}{/tex} and {tex}\cot y = \frac{3}{2}{/tex} Now, {tex}\cos x = \sqrt {1 - {{\sin }^2}x}{/tex} {tex}= \sqrt {1 - \frac{9}{{25}}} = \sqrt {\frac{{16}}{{25}}} = \frac{4}{5}{/tex} And {tex}\tan x = \frac{{\sin x}}{{\cos x}} = \frac{3}{4}{/tex} and {tex}\tan y = \frac{1}{{\cot y}} = \frac{2}{3}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \tan \left( {{{\sin }^{ - 1}}\frac{3}{5} + {{\cot }^{ - 1}}\frac{3}{2}} \right){/tex} = tan(x + y) {tex} = \frac{{\tan x + \tan y}}{{1 - \tan x\tan y}} = \frac{{\frac{3}{4} + \frac{2}{3}}}{{1 - \frac{3}{4} \times \frac{2}{3}}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{\frac{{17}}{{12}}}}{{\frac{1}{2}}} = \frac{{17}}{6}{/tex}
solution_html:
Putting, {tex}{\sin ^{ - 1}}\frac{3}{5} = x{/tex} and {tex}{\cot ^{ - 1}}\frac{3}{2} = y{/tex} so that {tex}\sin x = \frac{3}{5}{/tex} and {tex}\cot y = \frac{3}{2}{/tex}
Now, {tex}\cos x = \sqrt {1 - {{\sin }^2}x}{/tex} {tex}= \sqrt {1 - \frac{9}{{25}}} = \sqrt {\frac{{16}}{{25}}} = \frac{4}{5}{/tex}
And {tex}\tan x = \frac{{\sin x}}{{\cos x}} = \frac{3}{4}{/tex}
and {tex}\tan y = \frac{1}{{\cot y}} = \frac{2}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \tan \left( {{{\sin }^{ - 1}}\frac{3}{5} + {{\cot }^{ - 1}}\frac{3}{2}} \right){/tex}
= tan(x + y)
{tex} = \frac{{\tan x + \tan y}}{{1 - \tan x\tan y}} = \frac{{\frac{3}{4} + \frac{2}{3}}}{{1 - \frac{3}{4} \times \frac{2}{3}}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{\frac{{17}}{{12}}}}{{\frac{1}{2}}} = \frac{{17}}{6}{/tex}

solution_text: We need to prove that {tex}\sin ^ { - 1 } \frac { 8 } { 17 } + \sin ^ { - 1 } \frac { 3 } { 5 } = \tan ^ { - 1 } \frac { 77 } { 36 }{/tex} Let us consider, {tex}\sin ^ { - 1 } \frac { 8 } { 17 } = x \text { and } \sin ^ { - 1 } \frac { 3 } { 5 } = y , \quad x , y \in \left[ - \frac { \pi } { 2 } , \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \sin x = \frac { 8 } { 17 } \text { and } \sin y = \frac { 3 } { 5 }{/tex} Now, {tex}\cos ^ { 2 } x = 1 - \sin ^ { 2 } x = 1 - \frac { 64 } { 289 } = \frac { 225 } { 289 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cos x = \sqrt { \frac { 225 } { 289 } } = \frac { 15 } { 17 }{/tex} [taking positive square root as x {tex}x \in \left[ - \frac { \pi } { 2 } , \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right]{/tex} ] and {tex}\cos ^ { 2 } y = 1 - \sin ^ { 2 } y = 1 - \frac { 9 } { 25 } = \frac { 16 } { 25 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cos y = \sqrt { \frac { 16 } { 25 } } = \frac { 4 } { 5 }{/tex} [taking positive square root as {tex}x \in \left[ - \frac { \pi } { 2 } , \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right]{/tex} ] Clearly, tan x = {tex}\frac { \sin x } { \cos x } = \frac { 8 } { 15 } \text { and } \tan y = \frac { 3 } { 4 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x = \tan ^ { - 1 } \frac { 8 } { 15 } \text { and } y = \tan ^ { - 1 } \frac { 3 } { 4 }{/tex} Now, in general we can write {tex}\mathrm { LHS } = x + y = \tan ^ { - 1 } \frac { 8 } { 15 } + \tan ^ { - 1 } \frac { 3 } { 4 }{/tex} {tex}= \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { \frac { 8 } { 15 } + \frac { 3 } { 4 } } { 1 - \frac { 8 } { 15 } \times \frac { 3 } { 4 } } \right) = \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { 32 + 45 } { 60 - 24 } \right){/tex} {tex}= \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { 77 } { 36 } \right) = R H S{/tex} Hence proved.

solution_text: {tex} \tan ^ { - 1 } \left[ \frac { \sqrt { 1 + x } - \sqrt { 1 - x } } { \sqrt { 1 + x } + \sqrt { 1 - x } } \right] = \frac { \pi } { 4 } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \cos ^ { - 1 } x{/tex} Let LHS = {tex} \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { \sqrt { 1 + x } - \sqrt { 1 - x } } { \sqrt { 1 + x } + \sqrt { 1 - x } } \right){/tex} Put x = {tex} \cos 2 \theta , {/tex} {tex}\therefore \theta = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \cos ^ { - 1 } x{/tex} {tex} \therefore \quad \mathrm { LHS } = \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { \sqrt { 1 + \cos 2 \theta } - \sqrt { 1 - \cos 2 \theta } } { \sqrt { 1 + \cos 2 \theta } + \sqrt { 1 - \cos 2 \theta } } \right){/tex} {tex} = \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { \sqrt { 2 } \cos \theta - \sqrt { 2 } \sin \theta } { \sqrt { 2 } \cos \theta + \sqrt { 2 } \sin \theta } \right){/tex} {tex} \left[ \because 1 + \cos 2 \theta = 2 \cos ^ { 2 } \theta , 1 - \cos 2 \theta = 2 \sin ^ { 2 } \theta \right]{/tex} {tex} = \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { 1 - \tan \theta } { 1 + \tan \theta } \right){/tex} [dividing numerator and denominator by {tex} \sqrt { 2 } \cos \theta{/tex} ] {tex} = \tan ^ { - 1 } \left[ \tan \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } - \theta \right) \right]{/tex} {tex} \left[ \because \frac { 1 - \tan \theta } { 1 + \tan \theta } = \tan \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } - \theta \right) \right]{/tex} {tex} = \frac { \pi } { 4 } - \theta{/tex} {tex} = \frac { \pi } { 4 } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \cos ^ { - 1 } x{/tex} [ from equation (1) ] = RHS Hence Proved
solution_html:
{tex} \tan ^ { - 1 } \left[ \frac { \sqrt { 1 + x } - \sqrt { 1 - x } } { \sqrt { 1 + x } + \sqrt { 1 - x } } \right] = \frac { \pi } { 4 } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \cos ^ { - 1 } x{/tex}
Let LHS = {tex} \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { \sqrt { 1 + x } - \sqrt { 1 - x } } { \sqrt { 1 + x } + \sqrt { 1 - x } } \right){/tex}
Put x = {tex} \cos 2 \theta , {/tex}

{tex}\therefore \theta = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \cos ^ { - 1 } x{/tex}
{tex} \therefore \quad \mathrm { LHS } = \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { \sqrt { 1 + \cos 2 \theta } - \sqrt { 1 - \cos 2 \theta } } { \sqrt { 1 + \cos 2 \theta } + \sqrt { 1 - \cos 2 \theta } } \right){/tex}
{tex} = \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { \sqrt { 2 } \cos \theta - \sqrt { 2 } \sin \theta } { \sqrt { 2 } \cos \theta + \sqrt { 2 } \sin \theta } \right){/tex}
{tex} \left[ \because 1 + \cos 2 \theta = 2 \cos ^ { 2 } \theta , 1 - \cos 2 \theta = 2 \sin ^ { 2 } \theta \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { 1 - \tan \theta } { 1 + \tan \theta } \right){/tex}
[dividing numerator and denominator by {tex} \sqrt { 2 } \cos \theta{/tex}]
{tex} = \tan ^ { - 1 } \left[ \tan \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } - \theta \right) \right]{/tex}

{tex} \left[ \because \frac { 1 - \tan \theta } { 1 + \tan \theta } = \tan \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } - \theta \right) \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \frac { \pi } { 4 } - \theta{/tex}

{tex} = \frac { \pi } { 4 } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \cos ^ { - 1 } x{/tex}  [ from equation (1) ]

= RHS
Hence Proved

solution_text: Put {tex}x = \tan \theta{/tex} {tex}{\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{{1 - x}}{{1 + x}}} \right) = \frac{1}{2}{\tan ^{ - 1}}x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{{1 - \tan \theta }}{{1 + \tan \theta }}} \right) = \frac{1}{2}{\tan ^{ - 1}}\tan \theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{{\tan \frac{\pi }{4} - \tan \theta }}{{\tan \frac{\theta }{4} + \tan \theta }}} \right) = \frac{1}{2}\theta{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {\tan ^{ - 1}}\tan \left( {\frac{\pi }{4} - \theta } \right) = \frac{\theta }{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\pi }{4} - \theta = \frac{\theta }{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \theta + \frac{\theta }{2} = \frac{\pi }{4}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{3\theta }}{2} = \frac{\pi }{4}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 12\theta = 2\pi{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \theta = \frac{\pi }{6}{/tex} {tex}\therefore x = \tan \theta = \tan \frac{\pi }{6} = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex}
solution_html:
Put {tex}x = \tan \theta{/tex}
{tex}{\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{{1 - x}}{{1 + x}}} \right) = \frac{1}{2}{\tan ^{ - 1}}x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{{1 - \tan \theta }}{{1 + \tan \theta }}} \right) = \frac{1}{2}{\tan ^{ - 1}}\tan \theta{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{{\tan \frac{\pi }{4} - \tan \theta }}{{\tan \frac{\theta }{4} + \tan \theta }}} \right) = \frac{1}{2}\theta{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow {\tan ^{ - 1}}\tan \left( {\frac{\pi }{4} - \theta } \right) = \frac{\theta }{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\pi }{4} - \theta = \frac{\theta }{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \theta + \frac{\theta }{2} = \frac{\pi }{4}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{3\theta }}{2} = \frac{\pi }{4}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 12\theta = 2\pi{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \theta = \frac{\pi }{6}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore x = \tan \theta = \tan \frac{\pi }{6} = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex}
